solution: 
```java
class MyCalendarThree {
    TreeMap<Integer, Integer> timeline;

    public MyCalendarThree() {
        timeline = new TreeMap<>();
    }

    public int book(int start, int end) {
        timeline.put(start, timeline.getOrDefault(start, 0) + 1);
        timeline.put(end, timeline.getOrDefault(end, 0) - 1);
        int k = 0, curr = 0;
        for (int val : timeline.values()) {
            curr += val;
            k = Math.max(k, curr);
        }
        return k;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(log n)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses a TreeMap to track the changes in the number of overlapping events at each start and end time. It increments the count at the start and decrements at the end. By iterating through the TreeMap values, it calculates the maximum number of overlapping events (k). The TreeMap ensures efficient insertion and retrieval.
